speaker: interviewer
text: --Berger in Chicago, Illinois. And this is December 1, 1995. I want to go back and get some details from what you just told us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, when you were taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: From home?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, from home. First of all, did you have any advance notice of what was going to take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No. I remember we were sleeping. It was midnight, I remember. Somebody knocked on the door with-- with guns. They knocked on the door. And I still-- no, I didn't have a maid anymore. Because they took everybody. Jewish people couldn't have maid anymore. So we were alone. I opened the door, and four men with the-- with the guns, they came. He says, get dressed. You are going. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a shock. He says, hurry, hurry, hurry! Get dressed. OK. I says, please, go in other room. He says, no! You have to get dressed, undressed and dressed. OK, I did. And I took my baby with the carriage. They let-- I says, can I use the carriage? Because she was small.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, OK. You can-- you can take it. And they took us to truck and brought us to a-- to a place, a big warehouse, where all-- we were together, all Jewish people who were taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was horrible. You know, in the night, you sleep without notice, nothing, leave everything. Go. Where? We didn't know. So we came there and we-- in the night. In the morning, they came and they took everything what you have. If you have money or jewelry, they-- they took everything from you. And they put us in the train. Those train for the-- how they call it? Cattle cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So they put us in the train, closed in. And we go. Where? We didn't know. They took us in Zagreb, in a big place, and united with all other-- many cities there, we were there. And they took us again in the train, and we went. We ask where we go. Nobody told us anything. And that's how we-- we came to the first-- Gospic. And I remember we came there. And the same night, they took my husband, me and baby, and one other couple. They took us back to Koprivnica, because I had a baby and sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know why they took us back. But they didn't let us go to the house. They let us stay again in this warehouse over the night. And they took us in train again and then brought us first in Gospic. But not in the camp, they put us in prison. And oh, God, when they put us in prison and closed the door, you know? We were about-- my husband, me and baby, and my girlfriend with her daughter and her in-laws, we were in-- in one room. And we heard crying and yelling, because other prisoners, they were beaten, terrible. And we were all night together and, you know, cling to each other, what's going to happen to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: But in morning, they took us from this prison. And they took us to-- to camp where I told you about. I met my family there. They were there already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's tragic, but it was the happiest moment for my life, when I saw my parents there. And they were so happy to see me, you know, and to see, first time, the grandchild. And we didn't do anything there, nothing at all. Just being there together. And what did I want to say? And then after, other camp, when they separate us, you know, the men, they went to Jasenovac. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was the worst camp. Because I don't know, maybe 10 or 15 people survived from thousands of people in this. Because they were all killed in this camp, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when we are there, my girlfriends, what I told you, my best girlfriends, who was Croatian and Serbian, she came to visit us, visit me, brought some food. But they didn't let her in. And she begged. I begged them that she should take the baby with her, that at least she would be-- have a home. But they didn't let. They didn't want nobody to take her. She had to stay with me. And then about this Jasenovac, we didn't know where they went. That's all I find out later, after I was liberated. They put them in this Jasenovac. The older men, they all were killed. The younger one, they put to work. And they told me that my husband, they put him in a-- for the dog, what they have dog, this-- a doghouse. They put him in-- he couldn't even go and crawl like that-- and kept him for a week. And they didn't give him any-- any food, they said. And he was crying and yelling. And they says they took him from the-- from the stock house and say, go on your feet and you have to bark. Then you're going to have it. And they said he barked, but they kill him. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then my father, everybody could see that they always, when they did something, everybody had to be around and look. You know? And when he started-- my father started to cry, and they said, oh, you cry for him? OK, go around and run.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he did. And running, they kill him. And that's all what I heard about that, what happened. And I didn't hear anything about my brother but that he was killed. And how long he was living, I don't have any idea. I don't have any idea about that. I don't have any idea. And I-- I remember when, from Kruscica, when they took us to Zagreb, we were all praying, maybe they are taking us-- because they were-- we were told that the men sat in Jasenovac. Then we were told. And we said, oh, thank god, we're going to go to Jasenovac. So we're going to be reunited. But they didn't. They didn't. They took us in [NON-ENGLISH]. And like I said, I escaped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, they took me from the train, you know? And after baby died, I told you what happened, that I went from one, slept, you know. And how did I escape from Yugoslavia?

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend of mine, she had some connection. And he says they can give us papers, false paper. But we have to pay them. So I had a beautiful bracelet, and I gave it to him. And we get our paper and-- for both of us, because she didn't have any money. He was-- her father was a rabbi in Koprivnica. So we were like sister. We didn't have-- you know, we were so close then. And we came together to Crikvenica. It was on Adriatic Sea. We were there, I don't know, a couple of months, till I told you what happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask two questions. First, who was-- what was the name of your friend that you escaped with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who did?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your friend that you escaped with? Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. She's still living. She's in Koprivnica now, and I'm all the time in touch with her. Her name is Erna, Erna-- oh, what was-- Wolfenson, Erna Wolfenson. Now, her name is Relege, because she remarried. She married. She was a young girl then, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The second question is, who did you obtain-- who did you get the false papers from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. Because that was-- nobody wanted to tell, you know? One gave to the other one, because nobody wanted to be involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a partisan organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Partisan. Yes, the partisan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the name of the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Because that was a boo-boo, you know? You cannot-- no. I didn't know. I remember, I wanted to go to partisan. I wanted to go to fight, but they didn't let me. He says, you are too fragile. You cannot go. Because-- they didn't let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the three camps that you were in, and escaping to with the false papers, that was all in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in '41, yeah. In '41, yeah. When the Nazi-- I told you, when they come one day, that was-- you know when they came? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On Pesach. I remember on Pesach, they came. We were all down in the basement, because they were bombarding. And all of a sudden, we saw the tank coming, the German tank. And my God, I remember on-- I was-- where I was living, on a main street, I saw my next-door neighbor. And he took his hand, and it was Hakenkreuz. It was-- I almost died. My next-door neighbor, who always kissed my hand, and we played cards together. We had parties together, and always-- and I saw him there. And when he saw me next day, because he was next-door neighbor, he spit. I remember that. He spit. That was a terrible experience. But then I have the doctor who delivered my baby, he was such a nice human being. And he says, you know, if you have something, money or something, I'll be glad to-- my wife would hide it for you. So she did, my bracelet, you know. She had it by her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after I came from the camp, I went to them. Naturally, she gave me my bracelet. And I-- I was there for eight days, but I was so unhappy. Because I was full of louse, and I was so afraid that she-- I'm going to leave her louse. And I told her. She says, don't worry. Don't worry.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were just wonderful to me, just wonderful. I met them how many years back, when I was first time in Yugoslavia. In '74, I went to Koprivnica, and I met those people. They were old people already. And God, that was reunion.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't know how-- I says, you know what you did for me? He says, oh, they were so happy that I am in life. Because they didn't know what happened to me. Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Doctor-- oh, I cannot remember the name. Doctor-- I cannot remember the name. Is that terrible?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot remember their name. But they were most wonderful Croatian people. Just wonderful. My girlfriend told me that they both died about four years ago. I felt so bad, because they were so good. They were so good. There were lots of good people. Not everybody was bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, like they said, I hate Croatian. I hated them. But then later, I said, I hate them? Then we're like-- like Nazis, I'm hating. They're not all bad people. Some of them were very good people, and they are very good people. But unfortunately, those Ustase were very bad. Very, very bad. They were really like animals. When you were in the camp with the Ustase, the one that you were in that you said was on the mountain--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any dreams for the future? Did you have any idea if you would get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We-- we thought we're going to go-- nobody thought what's really happening. You know, you were living like in a-- I remember sometimes we were even singing. We were singing, from desperation. We were singing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you singing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And like I said, we talk about what we're going to do when we come out, how are we going to be good to each other, and how we're going to-- we never will be hungry anymore, and we would appreciate everything in life. I remember talking about that. Nobody really believed what happened. And when we left Croatia, so when they took me from the train, my parents-- I mean, my sisters and my mother went to this camp. They were there I don't know how many months. And they-- they took them to Auschwitz, and that's the last I know. What the last I was told, that was the first transport in Auschwitz. They went straight to gas chamber. That's what I was told. But I-- then I was told the young girls, they took young girls for the whore house. And I always-- my younger sister, I'm going to show you the picture. She was 16. She was beautiful girl. I thought, oh my god, I hope not her. I says, then if she have to die, better die in-- in gas chamber than doing that. And you know, it haunts me till now, the gas chamber and about my sister, her fate, what-- it haunts me. I still-- it's not a night that I don't think about. Now it's not so bad anymore. But in beginning, I was dreaming all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I imagined her-- you know, how they died, how they put this gas. Because I saw the movies after the war, you know? And it was so vivid. And I still remember. I still cannot get out of my mind. And then I had a guilt feeling. I says, why me? Why everybody else? Why I am living? What did I deserve, to-- to be alive? It's a very funny feeling, you know? Very funny feeling. But then I thought, it's our destiny. It must have been destiny that I'm alive. Because I was so many times, like I said, three times that I didn't want my baby. And really, she saved my life, my baby. I wouldn't be taken if not for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when I slept by some other places, every time I didn't sleep, they were taken. So it must be something. I'm not-- but I believe in that. I believe in destiny. It was destiny that I am alive. And I know now why, because now I have other daughter. And that's the best thing what ever happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that, we're going to talk later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. I want to talk about escaping to the Italian-occupied territory.

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Escaping to the Italian-occupied territory.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Italian?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were wonderful to us. In the camp, they were very good to us. They always told us, is they put us in the camp to protect us from Germany. Because you know, Mussolini was with Hitler, allied.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I don't know why, but he wanted to save us. He didn't want-- he says, OK, you want them to camp? I'm going to make camp for-- for them. So that's why they put us in concentration camp. But they treat us-- they treat us-- we couldn't go out. We were, you know, closed with wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were treated very nicely. We were treated very nicely there. And then, after Liberation, when we came to Italy, the Italian are more wonderful people. I have such regards for them. They were so good hearted. I remember one incident when we came-- we came without nothing. Because when we, again, escape from the partisan-- because when we were in-- after Liberation-- a very little town. One morning, we woke up, and we saw Germans. They came. I thought we're going to die this time. And we stayed in a little hotel. And the lady, she was a Serbian, and her husband was Croatian. He didn't know who we are. But she says, she felt that she knew that we are who we are, that we are Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she says, Zora, you know what? You have to escape. Because they are going to take you. So she took us to a little island in the night, and they put us on a little boat. And we went to the other city, where the partisans were, over there. And we came there. Didn't know where to go. And the partisans said, who are you? And we told them that we were in concentration camp, and that we wanted to-- to go to partisan zone and go to Italy. He says, no, you can't go. He says, how do we know who you are? Maybe you are spies. But somehow, they believe us. And they let us go. So we went again to the mountain. We came to a city. And we met friends who were with us in Italian camps. We met them. They were there. So that was a nice reunion. We were happy that we find some friends there. And we were there for one month, living wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then one night, they told us, Germans are coming. And in meantime, I was bitten by a dog. And I had a terrible infection in my right leg. I couldn't walk. The doctor told me, you cannot walk, because it's very bad. And when they told us, German come, my husband says, I have to go. He says, I'm going to leave you here. I have to go. I says, no, you won't leave me. I'm going. And I went with him to the mountain. I don't know how many hours we went. I hardly could walk. But we came, finally, to a-- to a house where the peasants were, and they took us. They were so good. They give us bread and milk, I remember. And I was sleeping on-- And they put me on the table, because they didn't have accommodations. They were just wonderful people. I remember them. And after a couple days, the partisan came. They says they were gone. It's safe to go back. And that was a New Year Day, I remember, New Year Night. It was snowing. And I went with this friend. She was an older lady with her son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And three of us, we took hands. And we went down, and we were singing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came there, but we didn't stay, because we were afraid. So we went to-- again in the night, with some little boat, till we reached a safe place before we came to Italy, where the Allies were there, you know? That was my experience, overall. You met your husband in the Italian camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Italian camp, yeah. We met in Italian camp, yeah. And after Liberation, we went together. Wherever we went, we went together. You know, we were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And like, I was the happiest person. I didn't care if I have to eat or not to eat, because I was so much in love and protected, you know? So when we came to Italy, then we got married there. First of all, what was your husband-- tell us your husband's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: What his name?

speaker: interviewer
text: His name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Julius Goldberger. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Julius Goldberger, that was his name. I remember, when we were married in city hall. And I had some friends. They were all witnesses. And we didn't have, even, a ring. My girlfriend took the ring so I could have, you know, my wedding. It was Friday. It was raining. And they took us, then, for a-- for a lunch. And that was that. But we had a wonderful--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: --time in Bari, because he had a very good job. And like I said, I had a very good job. And a year before we knew that-- oh, how we came to United States, because that's interesting too. Before we came, you had to have affidavit, because they didn't let us go otherwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my husband find a telephone book, and he took all Goldbergers. He wrote about, I don't know, 100 letters. I don't know how many letter, all over United States. And we got some replies, sorry, but they don't have any relatives in Europe, so they wish us luck, that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one family from Chicago, Alfred Goldberger, wrote us. It was beautiful letter. He says we are not related, because our grandparents already were born in United States. But from this day, we consider you like us, relative. And we're going to be happy to help you to send you the papers and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was going on-- we were waiting over a year, till we finally could get it. They send us beautiful clothing and packages. Every-- they were just wonderful. The letters, the pictures, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I was in Bari, I got pregnant. And I was so happy, because I wanted a child. And he wanted too. After three months, I remember, we were walking on the street. All of a sudden, I felt blood. I miscarriaged the baby in third month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then again, I got pregnant. And again, after three months, the doctor told me, you know what? Stay in bed. We're going to give you a shot every day, and you're going to carry the baby. I said, that's wonderful. So for three months, every day, the nurse came. She give me shot. And one day, I got-- you what is urticaria? Urticaria, that's some [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] allergic reaction. I got, all of a sudden, swollen. They had to come to cut my-- my ring.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't see. I was blind, completely swollen. I got like a poison in my system. And I remember I-- I was semi-conscious. Then one doctor who was living in the building, an Italian young guy, and he came. He says, we cannot lose anything. He tried. He gave me, I remember, such a big injection here, in my-- it was some water, some kind of distilled water, so the poison could go out. So for eight days, he gave me every day. And slowly, slowly, my infection was gone. And I remember a friend of mine was there this-- this day. And I says, oh, thank God. It's gone. I'm going to be mother. I says, nothing happened. And I went to washroom, all of a sudden, just flowing, the blood. They didn't have time to take me to the hospital. The doctor came. And I remember, on a small cocktail table, he did the-- you know. After that, I got very sick emotionally. And I lost so much blood. They took me to Rome, to reconvalescent home, a Jewish reconvalescent home. They took me there to recuperate. And I was there, maybe, a month. Then all of a sudden, I got pneumonia. But very bad. I got 104 fever. I was so sick. I was coughing. They took me in the hospital, and I was there three months. I remember every day, they took x-ray. I was in very bad shape. They feed-- they fed me, and-- and I had to have lots of honey and everything. And I gained about 30 pounds, because I was laying, and just they-- they wanted to feed me. My husband came every second week. He came from Bari to visit me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then after three months, they said that I'm OK, that I can go back to-- to Bari. He came. You know, he saw me only in the bed. When he saw me out, I didn't have what to wear. I-- nothing fit me. So we had to buy something. Then I came into Bari. Nobody recognized me. I looked terrible. 35 pounds, and I was so little and always slim. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after that, slowly, I recuperate. I was OK. Then we were told that our quota came, that we can go to United States. Good.
